meanings.definition: Able to withstand or recover quickly from difficult conditions.
meanings.exampleSentences: She is a resilient girl who quickly bounces back from setbacks.|The local economy has proven remarkably resilient.
meanings.whenToUse: Use when describing a person's character or a system's ability to handle stress.
meanings.usageNotes: Often used in psychological and economic contexts.

meanings.exampleSentences: Mobile phones are ubiquitous these days.|The company's logo has become ubiquitous in the city.
meanings.whenToUse: To describe something that is very common and seen everywhere.
meanings.usageNotes: A more formal alternative to 'common' or 'everywhere'.

meanings.definition: To say or do something that makes people feel more relaxed in a social situation.
meanings.exampleSentences: A joke can help to break the ice at a party.|The teacher organized a game to break the ice on the first day of class.
meanings.whenToUse: Specifically used for the beginning of a meeting or social gathering where people don't know each other well.

meanings.definition: To make something less severe, serious, or painful.
meanings.exampleSentences: Drainage schemes have helped to mitigate the effects of floods.|The government is trying to mitigate the impact of the rising cost of living.
meanings.whenToUse: Common in professional, legal, or environmental contexts regarding problems.
meanings.usageNotes: Different from 'solve'; it means making the bad situation better, not completely fixing it.

meanings.definition: Lasting for a very short time.
meanings.exampleSentences: Fame in the world of rock and roll is often ephemeral.|The colors of a sunset are beautiful but ephemeral.
meanings.whenToUse: To describe beauty, fame, or biological life cycles that don't last long.

meanings.definition: Dealing with things sensibly and realistically in a way that is based on practical rather than theoretical considerations.
meanings.exampleSentences: We need to take a pragmatic approach to the problem.|She is a pragmatic politician.
meanings.whenToUse: When discussing decision making or problem solving based on facts.

meanings.definition: Open to more than one interpretation; not having one obvious meaning.
meanings.exampleSentences: The wording of the agreement is ambiguous.|His reply to my question was somewhat ambiguous.
meanings.whenToUse: Describing language, instructions, or signs that are confusing or have multiple meanings.

meanings.definition: To do something in the easiest, cheapest, or fastest way, often by ignoring rules or quality.
meanings.exampleSentences: Don't cut corners when you're building a house.|The company was accused of cutting corners on safety.
meanings.whenToUse: Usually used as a criticism for poor quality work done too quickly.

meanings.definition: To make a problem, bad situation, or negative feeling worse.
meanings.exampleSentences: Her allergy was exacerbated by the dust.|The new law will only exacerbate the problem.
meanings.whenToUse: Common in medical, political, or social contexts to describe worsening conditions.

meanings.definition: Showing great attention to detail; very careful and precise.
meanings.exampleSentences: He was meticulous about his appearance.|The researcher kept meticulous records.
meanings.whenToUse: Use when praising someone's accuracy or thoroughness in their work.

meanings.definition: In a way that cannot be avoided.
meanings.exampleSentences: Inevitably, there was a certain amount of confusion.|The decision will inevitably lead to higher taxes.

meanings.definition: To examine or inspect closely and thoroughly.
meanings.exampleSentences: He scrutinized the document for any errors.|The bank will scrutinize your financial records before approving the loan.

meanings.definition: Unnecessary, especially through being more than enough.
meanings.exampleSentences: The report contained a lot of superfluous detail.|Our technician checked the wiring and replaced a few superfluous parts.

meanings.definition: Slightly unwell or in low spirits.
meanings.exampleSentences: I'm feeling a bit under the weather today, so I'll stay home.|He's been under the weather lately.
meanings.whenToUse: A polite, casual way to say you are slightly sick without being specific.

meanings.definition: Relating to the mental processes of perception, memory, judgment, and reasoning.
meanings.exampleSentences: Cognitive development in children is fascinating.|Sleep deprivation can impair cognitive function.

meanings.definition: To contemplate or conceive of as a possibility or a desirable future event.
meanings.exampleSentences: I don't envisage any problems with this plan.|It's hard to envisage how the city will look in 50 years.

meanings.definition: A thing that is required as a prior condition for something else to happen or exist.
meanings.exampleSentences: A degree is a prerequisite for this job.|Good health is a prerequisite for happiness.

meanings.definition: To make suffering, deficiency, or a problem less severe.
meanings.exampleSentences: The drugs will help to alleviate the pain.|The government is taking steps to alleviate poverty.

meanings.definition: A situation in which you can enjoy the advantages of two very different things at the same time.
meanings.exampleSentences: She lives in the country but works in the city, so she has the best of both worlds.|This hybrid car gives you the best of both worlds.
